What My Bank Balance Knows About Me

The Question

I tracked my spending to understand not just where my money goes, but also when and why I tend to spend. This story explores my spending habits across categories, time of day, mood, and transaction size to see what patterns show up in my behaviour.

What am I spending most on?

Most of my spending is concentrated in a few key categories rather than being evenly spread out. Food and drinks take up the largest share of my spending, followed by clothes and entertainment. Smaller categories like transport and bill/subscriptions make up much less overall.

This suggests that my spending is mostly driven by everyday lifestyle choices rather than fixed or essential costs.

Does mood affect how I spend?

The moods I loggest while spending…

My spending does seem to change depending on my mood. I tend to spend more when I’m feeling happy, especially on “want” purchases rather than “need” purchases.

When I’m bored or stressed, my spending is more likely to shift toward necessary items rather than optional or impulse purchases. One clear pattern is that higher spending often appears when I’m in a positive mood, which suggests emotions play a role in how freely I spend.

When do I spend the most money during the day?

Line plot showing total spending by hour of day, with a noticeable peak around 5pm.

My spending is not evenly distributed across the day. Instead, there is a noticeable peak around 5pm, where my total spending is highest.

This makes sense when I think about my routine, as its often when I have finished most of my day, have more free tim, or am casually scrolling on my phone. It also could reflect catching up purchases I forgot to log earlier in the day.

Overall, my spending seems to cluster in periods where I’m less busy and more relaxed.

Do I usually make small or large purchases

I make more small purchases here and there. large purchases are rare but significant.

Most of my transactions are small, everyday purchases, with only a few large spending events. This creates a pattern where frequent low-value purchases dominate my spending behaviour, while large purchases stand out as ocasional spikes.

Overall, my spending is mostly made up of frequent small purchases, with only a few large transactions making a big impact.

What this tells me overall

Looking at my spending patterns together, I can see that my spending behaviour is influence by routine, mood, and timing. Most of my spending is made up of small, frequent decisions rather than large planned purchases, and these decisions often cluster around certain times of the day.
